summary: Shaw recounts his experience learning digital painting during a six-hour flight, comparing it to traditional painting. He argues that digital painting tools fundamentally misunderstand color mixing by presenting infinite color pickers instead of simulating how painters actually combine pigments from limited palettes. Despite this major flaw, he finds digital painting useful for portability and cost, and describes his process of following along with Richard Robinson's painting videos using ArtRage on a cheap graphics tablet. He draws a parallel between his painting learning method and his coding education philosophy: forcing yourself to work from memory rather than side-by-side copying.
key_points: Digital painting tools fail at color mixing because they present infinite color pickers instead of simulating how painters actually combine limited primary colors
The learning method of switching between full-screen reference and full-screen workspace forces visual memory training, paralleling Shaw's approach to teaching coding
Digital painting's main advantages are portability, lower cost, and safety compared to traditional media with solvents and pigments
Pre-mixing and storing colors in a samples palette proved more effective than trying to mix colors on the fly in digital tools
Following along with painting videos that include the instructor's mistakes is valuable because you learn to fix errors alongside them
money_quotes: Most of the painting programs are in that weird uncanny valley where they're close, but so irritatingly far that they feel awkward and weird.
They seem to think 'mixing' means choosing a color from all the possible colors. That's not how a painter works. I make new colors by combining colors from two piles of paint.
My gamut is actually fairly small, which actually makes mixing paint color easier because I can narrow my palette of primary colors down.
Putting down strokes of color is not the difficult part. It's getting the color right that's hard.
Don't put them side-by-side as that doesn't exercise your ability to remember blocks of code. You'll learn faster if you have to cover what you're typing in while you type it.

No solvents, water, paint, paper, canvases, brushes, tubes of paint, paper towels, or any of the crap. Hell, it just takes all the fun out of getting into painting anyway. No more toys to buy. I should put this in the disadvantages column actually now that I think about it.
I also end up copying Richard's mistakes and then learning how to fix them, which is really helpful.
key_insight: Digital painting tools fundamentally misunderstand painters by offering infinite color selection instead of simulating the constrained, subtractive process of mixing from a limited palette of primaries.
